item.title: How the Middle East war is already impacting mortgage rates in Canada
item.description: The ongoing war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are impacting Canadian pocketbooks in ways we might not expect. Experts warn Canadian homeowners facing mortgage renewals that rates are going up, despite the Bank of Canada holding its key interest rate for months.

item.title: Could Trump follow through on his threats to quit NATO? Experts say it’s possible
item.description: OTTAWA - President Donald Trump's comments to a British newspaper this week revived speculation about whether he is ready to pull the U.S. out…

item.title: Alberta’s proposed immigration bill would create more red tape: hospitality sector
item.description: The hospitality sector says the bill requiring employers to be registered and be licensed to hire foreign workers creates more barriers when the industry is already struggling.

item.title: Doug Ford eyes a byelection win in Scarborough amid Liberal and NDP troubles
item.description: With the departure of New Democratic MPP Doly Begum to run for the federal Liberals, Ford has been closely watching developments in the riding.

item.title: Canada Soccer to offer Italy jersey swap for home team colours after FIFA World Cup elimination
B.C. First Nations criticize suspension of DRIPA as ‘absolute betrayal’
item.description: Leaked transcript of meeting between Indigenous leaders, Premier Eby shows them accusing him of colonialism

item.title: Champagne leaves China with pork tariffs still in place, but touts relationship-building
item.description: Although Chinese tariffs on Canadian pork products remain in place after a visit to China by Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne, he says the important task is relationship-building — and it's not realistic to expect immediate solutions.

item.title: Eglinton LRT hours extended past midnight
item.description: The change will come into service starting Sunday.

item.title: Advanced voting begins in 3 key byelections. What to know
item.description: The byelections this month are in two Liberal stronghold seats in the Toronto area and one contested riding in Quebec, north of Montreal.

item.title: Canada, China sign pledge in Beijing to deepen financial-sector ties
item.description: Canada’s Finance Minister says he raised the importance of labour standards during meetings with top Chinese officials

item.title: Ottawa unveils $51-million in additional aid for Ukraine
item.description: More than half will go toward humanitarian aid through outside organizations, and $5-million will go toward programming for veterans

item.title: Quebec law banning public prayer looms over Way of the Cross marches in province
item.description: Head of Assembly of Quebec Catholic Bishops says new secularism law turns religious people into ‘second-class citizens’

item.title: ‘Double the cuteness, double the fun’: Toronto Zoo welcomes two young goats
‘Not one, not two, but three otter pups:’ The first river otters born at the Toronto Zoo in over 40 years
Young Canadians are increasingly miserable. Government priorities show why
item.description: Governments have not matched the rise in spending on boomers with new revenue from that generation

item.title: Nate Erskine-Smith claims a nomination rival doesn’t live in Scarborough Southwest. ‘I’m not sure where he’s coming from,’ his opponent says
One year after the U.S. imposed tariffs on Canada's auto sector, here's how industry leaders say it's going
item.description: One year after U.S. President Donald Trump levied tariffs on the Canadian auto industry, industry leaders say they are eager to see an arrangement that restores predictability to the Canada-U.S. trade relationship.

item.title: Liberal bill would force companies to simplify data access for police. No one knows the cost
item.description: The Liberal government's second attempt at giving police and spies easier access to Canadians' information includes what's anticipated to be costly demands on a range of private businesses to to change how they manage their data.

item.title: Carney leans on private money, alternative approaches in nature strategy. Will it work?
item.description: The new approaches could provide an important boost to conservation in Canada and reduce the burden on Ottawa to do the work alone, but experts say if it’s not done correctly, these other-conserved areas may end up existing on paper but not providing the level of protection necessary for ecosystems to thrive.

item.title: Doug Ford praises Mark Carney and appeals for a majority government ahead of byelections
Canada urged to adopt U.S. approach to goods made with forced labour from China’s Xinjiang
item.description: Advocates ask Ottawa to toughen system, treat all products from the region as if they were tainted by forced labour unless importers can prove otherwise

item.title: Politics Insider: In Beijing, Canadian CEOs say they see business opportunities in China
item.description: Manulife and Sun Life say they see opportunities for growth in the Chinese market

item.title: Federal government saying little about Toronto's call to block ICE from World Cup
item.description: The federal government is not responding directly to a Toronto city council motion opposing the presence of any U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents at upcoming FIFA World Cup games.

item.title: Ottawa proposes making it easier to share personal data among government
item.description: In a new policy paper, the government proposes allowing federal agencies to reuse and share personal data, without consent, if it improves services or programs.

item.title: Federal privacy law changes would expand sharing of personal data across government
Pierre Poilievre takes to another U.S. podcast to sketch out his vision for Canada
Ford government giving sweeping powers to appointed regional chairs
Top Canadian bankers join finance minister on Chinese trade mission
item.description: Representatives from some of the country's most powerful financial institutions have joined a trade mission to China to promote Canada in one of the world's largest markets.

item.title: Tory MP echoes calls for Carney to apologize to Grassy Narrows over protest remarks
Michael Ford, the premier’s nephew, decides not to run for mayor of Toronto
Canada’s GDP outlook slashed by 20% over Iran war ‘uncertainty’
item.description: While the economy is still expected to grow modestly, the firm's spring economic outlook now estimates 1.2 per cent growth in 2026, down from the 1.5 per cent estimated in January.

item.title: Conservatives call for federal relief at the pumps as Iran war pushes prices up
item.description: OTTAWA - The Conservatives are calling on the Liberals to give Canadians some relief at the pumps by suspending federal taxes on gas and diese…

item.title: Candidate ends his bid for Conservative nomination in Poilievre’s former riding
Supreme Court won't hear case contesting constitutionality of Canada's voting system
item.description: The Supreme Court of Canada will not hear an appeal challenging the constitutionality of Canada's first-past-the-post electoral system.

item.title: Michael Ford says he’s giving ‘serious consideration’ to Toronto mayoral run
item.description: Michael Ford, a former Toronto city councillor and MPP who is the nephew of Ontario Premier Doug Ford, says he’s seriously considering tossing his hat in the ring to become the city’s next mayor.

item.title: Loss of language and reading programs at TCDSB ‘devastating,’ parents say
These Toronto City Hall workers voted on a union drive more than a year ago. Here’s why they’re still waiting
item.description: A group claiming to represent staff at city hall that voted in a union drive launched a publicity campaign Tuesday about their struggle.

item.title: Conservatives call for dropping tax on gas and diesel for rest of this year
item.description: Poilievre says lifting fuel excise tax, clean fuel standard and GST surcharges would save about 25 cents a litre

item.title: Poilievre calls for federal tax holiday on fuel as gas prices spike
item.description: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is calling on the government to temporarily pause federal taxes on gas and diesel for the remainder of the year.

item.title: To meet next NATO spending target, Canada needs a credible fiscal plan, think-tank says
item.description: After hitting NATO's two per cent GDP spending target in March 2026, Canada now faces a daunting climb to five per cent by 2035, according to the CD Howe Institute, one of the country’s leading economic think-tanks.

item.title: If court halts separatists' referendum bid, they'll push Danielle Smith to approve it anyway
item.description: A First Nation's injunction request, to be heard April 7, claims the independence petition drive threatens treaty rights.

item.title: Manulife, Sun Life CEOs see opportunities in China ahead of meeting with vice-premier
item.description: Finance Minister Champagne is in Beijing leading a delegation of Canadian business leaders pushing to boost trade

item.title: Scores of asylum claimants warned they may face deportation after immigration law passes
item.description: Letters warning of deportation sent immediately after Bill C-12 becomes law

item.title: Politics Insider: BoC Governor and Bay Street executives to join Champagne in Beijing
item.description: The group includes executives from Manulife, Sunlife and BMO Wealth Management

item.title: Federal 30-days-or-free policy for passports now in place
item.description: The federal government's new "30 days or free" policy for issuing passports takes effect on Wednesday.

item.title: Liberals planned to buy back 136,000 banned guns. Fewer than half that many were declared
item.description: The federal public safety minister's office says more than 67,000 assault-style firearms have been declared, far short of the 136,000 the government had budgeted for. The uptake combined with hurdles for the next stage are raising questions about whether the program will accomplish the government's goal of abolishing assault-style firearms in Canada.

item.title: Thousands of federal employees weighing early retirement offer
item.description: Thousands of federal public servants are currently mulling over a pivotal career decision: whether to accept or decline their employer's offer of an early retirement incentive.

item.title: Doug Ford takes anti-tariff message to the heart of Texas
Ontario minimum wage workers to get raise on Oct. 1. Here’s how much they’ll make
item.description: Ontario’s minimum wage is set to rise to $17.95 an hour on Oct. 1.

item.title: Ontario Autism Program spend now nearly $1B, advocates hope it makes dent in waitlist
Human rights advocate Stephen Lewis fought to alleviate suffering in Africa
item.description: In a life devoted to public service, he served as an eloquent and charismatic leader of Ontario’s NDP, and spent the latter part of his career in international roles working to end apartheid, curtail the spread of AIDS and investigate the causes of the Rwandan genocide

item.title: Expert’s report criticizes CRA over audit of Muslim charity, calls for improvements
Carney says he’s still committed to green incentives promised during leadership race
Ontario shuts down one way of funding nurse practitioners as province misses deadline
item.description: Ontario’s health minister is shutting down one avenue of publicly funding all nurse practitioners, as the province misses a federal deadline for ensuring their medically necessary services are covered.

item.title: Tens of thousands are expected to live on Toronto’s booming waterfront. Jets at Billy Bishop may change all that
First Nations chiefs demand apology after PM said he could ‘outlast’ protester
The countdown is on to Canada’s moon moment
item.description: The post The countdown is on to Canada’s moon moment appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: Minimum wage increase is not enough for life in Toronto, agency says
item.description: Raising it to $17.95 an hour is well below the $27.20 living wage required for survival, says the Ontario Living Wage Network.

item.title: Anand to join U.K.-led talks on reopening Strait of Hormuz without U.S.
item.description: Anand says Canada will not hesitate to help secure the strait once there is a ceasefire, but the Liberal government has not decided yet on specific measures.

item.title: Canada starts ’30 days or free’ guarantee for passport proceedings
item.description: The new guarantee will have the Canadian government refund fees if you wait for more than 30 business days for your passport to be processed.

item.title: Changes needed at Canada Revenue Agency after audit of Muslim charity: report
item.description: A report by an expert in extremist financing and money laundering says the Canada Revenue Agency's approach to policing terrorist abuse "proved seriously deficient" in the case of a long-running audit of the Muslim Association of Canada.

item.title: U.S. says Ottawa failing to block imports made with forced labour as Washington weighs more tariffs
item.description: Report an early indication of likely result in U.S. probe that could see new levies imposed

item.title: Stephen Lewis, longtime Ontario NDP politician and diplomat, dies at 88
item.description: The New Democratic stalwart's death was announced on Tuesday, just days after Lewis's son Avi was elected leader of the federal NDP.

item.title: Ottawa pressed regulator to give airlines a break on passenger compensation, records show
item.description: Regulator agreed to exempt carriers from cash penalties if flight disruptions arose from mechanical issues or labour strikes

item.title: Canada gives Ukrainians who fled war another year to apply for work permit extensions
Bank of Canada relying on ‘judgment’ to steer policy rate through Iran war shock
Trump says he’s considering pulling out of NATO, calls it a ‘paper tiger’
item.description: The 77-year-old U.S.-led military alliance was a 'paper tiger' and Russian President Vladimir Putin 'knows that too,' Trump said in an interview.

item.title: Stephen Lewis dead at 88: Canadian leaders share tributes to a ‘giant’ in the NDP movement
Ottawa renews funding for Toronto youth-crime prevention programs
item.description: The announcement by the federal government came on the same day the federal funding was due to run out.
